prompt:
The outbound webhook relay (relay.py, attached — works fine, ships events to every partner today) needs the routing upgrade we promised partners last quarter. Current call sites construct Relay(transport) and add_subscriber(id, url) positionally and must keep working exactly as-is; the EXISTING BEHAVIOR block in test_relay.py passes today and has to stay green.

Three additions, all pinned by the rest of test_relay.py (python3 test_relay.py fully green is the bar, test file read-only, stdlib only):

1. Per-subscriber event filters. add_subscriber grows an optional keyword events=None: a list of event-type patterns — an exact type like "invoice.paid", a prefix pattern like "order.*" (matches anything starting with "order.", and only with the dot — "orders.export" must NOT match), or a bare "*" for everything. None keeps today's everything behavior. The important part: an event a subscriber didn't ask for must not even CREATE a delivery record for them — the ops dashboard counts delivery rows and filtered noise would wreck the numbers.

2. Field-mapping transforms. add_subscriber also grows mapping=None: a dict of {outgoing_field: dotted.path.into.payload}. When set, the envelope's payload becomes exactly the mapped dict — each dotted path walks nested dicts in the event payload; a path that doesn't resolve (missing key, or a non-dict in the middle) just omits that outgoing field. Envelope id and event stay as they are. No mapping means the full payload, unchanged, like today.

3. Delivery attempt log + dead-lettering. Every send attempt gets logged, reachable as relay.attempts(delivery_id) -> list of {"n": 1-based attempt number, "ok": bool, "error": str(exception) if the transport raised else None} (unknown delivery id -> []). Relay grows an optional keyword max_attempts=None: after that many FAILED attempts a delivery flips to status "dead_letter", is never handed to the transport again, and shows up in relay.dead_letters(). Default None keeps today's retry-forever behavior. Delivered/dead_letter are terminal; dispatch()'s return value stays what it is now (deliveries completed in that run).

Don't turn this into an HTTP client — the transport stays an injected callable, that's deliberate (tests script it with successes, declines, and raises).
